text:
Modern steganography for digital media primarily relies on embedding payloads while minimizing a carefully defined distortion function. With the development of practical codes that operate near the theoretical payload-distortion bound, the central challenge for the steganographer has shifted toward designing distortion functions that offer high resistance to empirical statistical detection. 

In this paper, we introduce Universal Wavelet Relative Distortion (UNIWARD), a universal distortion design applicable across multiple embedding domains. The distortion is calculated as the sum of relative changes in coefficients within a directional filter bank decomposition of the cover image. By utilizing directionality, UNIWARD concentrates embedding changes in regions that are difficult to model across multiple directions—such as textures and noisy areas—while intentionally avoiding smooth regions and clean edges. 

We evaluate the performance of UNIWARD-based methods in the spatial, JPEG, and side-informed JPEG domains using both state-of-the-art rich models and targeted attacks. Our experimental results demonstrate that UNIWARD consistently matches or exceeds the security of current state-of-the-art steganographic methods across all tested domains.
